name: Québec
contextBlurb: Québec sits in an unusual split: an AI innovation champion and a grid-constrained energy regulator at the same time. Clean hydropower and heavy Mila institute subsidies attracted Google, AWS, and OVHcloud to the Beauharnois corridor, while Bill 69 (2025) now restricts new 5+ MW industrial power connections and effectively pauses new data center buildouts. Law 25 — Canada's strictest provincial privacy regime — imposes GDPR-like automated decision-making rules backed by fines up to $25M or 4% of global revenue.

legislation.title: An Act to ensure the responsible governance of energy resources and to amend various legislative provisions
legislation.summary: Enacted June 7, 2025, this law requires any new electricity load of 5 MW or more — including data centers — to obtain ministerial authorization from the Minister of Economy, Innovation and Energy, with approval conditioned on technical feasibility, economic benefit, and environmental impact. It effectively gates all large-scale data center power connections through direct government discretion, continuing and codifying restrictions that began with Bill 2 in 2023.

legislation.title: Act to modernize legislative provisions as regards the protection of personal information (Loi modernisant des dispositions législatives en matière de protection des renseignements personnels)
legislation.summary: Fully in effect since September 2024, Law 25 imposes GDPR-style obligations on all organizations processing personal data of Québec residents, including mandatory Privacy Impact Assessments for AI systems, explicit disclosure when decisions are made exclusively through automated processing (Section 12.1), and penalties up to $25 million CAD or 4% of worldwide turnover. The Commission d'accès à l'information (CAI) is ramping up enforcement audits in 2026, making it Canada's strictest provincial AI privacy framework.

legislation.title: Québec Government $36M AI Research and Talent Investment (Mila)
legislation.summary: Announced February 27, 2026, the Québec government committed $36 million CAD to Mila – Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute to strengthen its university excellence network, accelerate AI adoption in business, and attract global AI talent to the province. This investment reflects the provincial government's strategy to position Québec as an ethical and responsible AI leader globally, complementing Mila's concurrent $100M USD venture fund with Inovia Capital.

legislation.title: Hydro-Québec Data Centre Tariff (Régie de l'énergie, late 2026)
legislation.summary: Hydro-Québec filed a new electricity-rate structure for data centres at the Régie de l'énergie setting ~13¢/kWh — roughly double current large-power tariffs — for facilities consuming more than 5 MW per year. The tariff is expected to take effect in the second half of 2026 and applies to all new data-centre customers, alongside continued ministerial authorisation under Bill 69.

legislation.title: Quebec Law 25 — 2026 AI and Cross-Border Enforcement Push
legislation.summary: The Commission d'accès à l'information signalled in early 2026 that routine compliance audits under Law 25 §70 would increase, especially around AI-driven automated decision-making (§12.1, §14) and cross-border transfers outside Quebec (§17). Penalties reach C$10 million or 2% of worldwide turnover, with repeat offences up to C$25 million.

legislation.title: Bill 69 — Ministerial Authorisation for New Loads ≥ 5 MW
legislation.summary: Enacted June 2025 and operationalised through 2026, Bill 69 requires the Minister of Economy, Innovation and Energy to authorise any new electricity load of 5 MW or more — directly capturing AI data centres. Combined with Hydro-Québec's proposed doubled tariff, it effectively halts speculative data-centre build-out unless projects deliver clear economic benefit.
